brief_justification: The correct answer is self-organization, not emergence — the agent inverts the hierarchy, claiming self-organization is a subtype of emergence when the question maps directly to self-organization as the structural principle; the agent identifies relevant properties (local-only information, no central controller, simple rules, global coherence) which goes one level beyond the surface; the agent never questions its own hierarchy claim or considers that self-organization might be the target term rather than a subtype; no counterfactual check, no boundary testing, and no re-derivation from the examples to validate the emergence-over-self-organization claim; the agent asserts the hierarchy with confidence and no uncertainty markers despite this being a contested taxonomic question in complexity science; self-organization is mentioned but immediately dismissed as a subtype rather than genuinely evaluated as the candidate answer; the claim that self-organization is a subtype of emergence is asserted without citation, derivation, or logical chain, leaving no reproducible audit trail for that key premise.
